subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the performance of the per-inode writeback approach, specifically that it regresses on multi-writer and random write workloads due to lost I/O batching. The author acknowledges this regression and suggests sticking with v2, which has a different approach.
reviews.raw_body:
Actually, that version regressed performance in a couple of cases. I think v2 is probably the best approach, on balance. Maybe we can get this into -next so that it can make v7.2?

Here's the comparison between this version and a writeback_single_inode() flush version:

------------------8<-----------------------

● Comparing dontcache numbers against the previous whole-BDI flusher kernel (from /tmp/dontcache-local-4way-flusher.md):                                                                          

  Per-Inode vs Whole-BDI Flusher — DONTCACHE on Local XFS                                                                                                                                         

  Single-Client Writes

  ┌──────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬─────────────┐                                                                                                                                      
  │    Benchmark     │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change    │                                                                                                                                      
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤      
  │ Seq write MB/s   │ 1450      │ 1438      │ -1% (noise) │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Seq write p99.9  │ 23.5 ms   │ 23.5 ms   │ identical   │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤                                                                                                                                      
  │ Rand write MB/s  │ 363       │ 286       │ -21%        │
  ├──────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤                                                                                                                                      
  │ Rand write p99.9 │ 1.8 ms    │ 16.7 ms   │ regression  │
  └──────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴─────────────┘

  Seq write is identical. Rand write regressed — the whole-BDI flusher batched all dirty pages in one pass with writeback_sb_inodes() under a single blk_plug, while per-inode write_inode_now() loses that batching.

  Single-Client Reads

  ┌────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────┐
  │   Benchmark    │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │ Change │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────┤
  │ Seq read MB/s  │ 2950      │ 2350      │ -20%   │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────┤
  │ Rand read MB/s │ 651       │ 519       │ -20%   │
  └────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────┘

  Reads shouldn't be affected by writeback path changes. Buffered reads also dropped (2888 → 2331), suggesting different system conditions between runs rather than a per-inode regression.

  Multi-Writer (Scenario A)

  ┌────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬────────────┐
  │     Metric     │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change   │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┤
  │ Aggregate MB/s │ 1478      │ 999       │ -32%       │
  ├────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼────────────┤
  │ p99.9          │ 46 ms     │ 77 ms     │ -67% worse │
  └────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴────────────┘

  This is the biggest regression. With whole-BDI, the flusher did one batched pass through all dirty inodes via writeback_sb_inodes(). With per-inode, each of 4 writers queues a separate work item processed serially by write_inode_now() — losing the batch I/O merging benefit.

  Scenario C & D (Noisy Neighbor)

  ┌─────────────────────────┬───────────┬───────────┬─────────────┐
  │         Metric          │ Whole-BDI │ Per-Inode │   Change    │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario C writer       │ 1468      │ 1386      │ -6%         │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario C readers      │ 18.7 MB/s │ 18.7 MB/s │ identical   │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D writer       │ 1472      │ 1467      │ identical   │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D readers      │ 496 MB/s  │ 507 MB/s  │ +2%         │
  ├─────────────────────────┼───────────┼───────────┼─────────────┤
  │ Scenario D reader p99.9 │ 440 us    │ 358 us    │ +19% better │
  └─────────────────────────┴───────────┴───────────┴─────────────┘

  Mixed-mode (Scenario D) is the intended production case and it's essentially identical or slightly better — per-inode writeback creates less device contention for buffered readers.

  Summary

  The per-inode approach is neutral-to-slightly-better for the production scenario (Scenario D), but regresses on multi-writer and random write workloads. The core issue is loss of I/O batching
 — writeback_sb_inodes() processes all dirty inodes in one blk_plug'd pass, while per-inode write_inode_now() calls are processed one at a time. The read regressions likely reflect different
  system conditions since buffered/direct reads also dropped ~20%.

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary: Christoph Hellwig noted that the current implementation of wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi causes WB_SYNC_NONE writeback to occur every time a DONTCACHE write finishes, which is inefficient and unnecessary. He suggested introducing a new WB_start_dontcache bit, a get_nr_dontcache_pages() helper function, and other related changes.
reviews.raw_body:
wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi ends up calling wb_start_writeback eventually,
which sets WB_start_all, pushes the reason to start_all_reason and then
does the actual wakeup.

The flusher thread then through wb_check_start_all does a WB_SYNC_NONE
writeback based on get_nr_dirty_pages.  Which seems wrong - we don't
want to do a huge writeback evertime the some DONTCACHE write finished.

So I think you'll want a new WB_start_dontcache bit, a new
get_nr_dontcache_pages() helper on a new node counter, etc.

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary: Reviewer Christoph Hellwig requested that the extra reason arguments for wide wakeups not be exposed outside the core writeback code, and also suggested marking wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi static in fs-writeback.c
reviews.raw_body:
I'd rather not expose that to wide with the extra reason arguments
that's not otherwise exposed outside the core writeback code.

Btw, wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi should really be marked static in
fs-writeback.c as well.

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the benchmark results provided in the patch description are incomplete and potentially stale, suggesting they be included directly in the patch for better reproducibility. Additionally, he questioned the validity of certain benchmark numbers, specifically the read changes between patches and unpatched dontcache in XFS and NFSD, requesting further investigation into their causes.
reviews.raw_body:
Please add the results into the patch.  Besides having them in the patch
vs the cover letter, URLs to random hosting sites tend to get stale
very easily.

Comments on the XFS numbers / benchmark setup:

How does O_DIRECT manage to create almost the same peek dirty as
buffered?  Similarly even this patched doncache show be lower, which
feels odd.

(the editorializing in these results feels odd, not sure what tool
generates them, but maybe edit it for the commit log to look a bit more
serious).

Given that this patch does not change the read path, how do the pure read
numbers for patches vs unpatched dontcache manage to differ so much?

Comments on the NFSD numbers / benchmark setup:

I though you were testing using dontcache on the server, but the
comments seem to imply it is done on the client, what is the case
here? 

Again, the read changes between patches and unpatched look suspect.
Please drill down why they happen, the fact that patches is slower here
and faster in XFS makes me wonder if the results might just be very
volatile?

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that limiting writeback to a specific inode has several drawbacks, which explains the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE in write-heavy workloads.

Reviewer expressed concern about the timing of the patchset's submission, noting that targeting 7.2 is not a priority and that the merge window for 7.1 is closing soon, also requesting input from Jens
reviews.raw_body:
Limiting writeback to a specific inode has many downsides as I explained
before, so this is not surprising.

---

If you want to target 7.2 we're not in any rush.  If you were trying to
say 7.1 we're way to close too the merge window.  We also haven't
heard from Jens at all, whom I'd really like to look over this.

2026-04-08.patch_summary: This patch set improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by kicking the flusher thread for the superblock instead of initiating writeback directly. This change is based on benchmarking results that show a clear improvement over the status quo, particularly in cases with heavy write activity. The patch also includes a comparison of different IO modes for nfsd and highlights the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE with write-heavy workloads. The approach taken involves modifying the generic_write_sync function to kick the flusher thread, which is expected to better isolate the DONTCACHE writeback behavior and improve overall performance.

reviews.author: Ritesh (IBM)
reviews.summary: Ritesh pointed out that the current API gives a misleading impression of kicking writeback for only an inode's address space mapping, when in fact it starts writeback for all dirty pages on the respective bdi. He suggested exporting the symbol for wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi() and using it instead, or alternatively modifying filemap_kick_writeback_all(mapping, enum wb_reason) to better reflect its behavior.
reviews.raw_body:
This api gives a wrong sense that we are kicking writeback to write
dirty pages which belongs to only this inode's address space mapping.
But instead we are starting wb for everything on the respective bdi.

So instead why not just export symbol for wakeup_flusher_threads_bdi()
and use it instead?

If not, then IMO at least making it... 
   filemap_kick_writeback_all(mapping, enum wb_reason)

... might be better.

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary:
Jan Kara noted that the patch can lead to performance regressions under certain workloads, specifically when a process creates a large temporary file and deletes it shortly after creation while another process does small DONTCACHE writes. This results in unnecessary allocation, writeback, and deletion of the temporary file.

Reviewer Jan Kara expressed concerns about the patch's approach to writeback and suggested two alternative solutions: either marking inodes with dropbehind set and restricting flush worker to only write such inodes, or lazily clearing an 'inode has dropbehind folios' flag once the inode is clean.
reviews.raw_body:
This is all true and I can certainly construct a workload where this
behavior will lead to big regressions in performance - for example one
process creating say 1G temporary file and deleting it shortly after
creation + one process doing small DONTCACHE writes. Before this change
only DONTCACHE writes will make it to the disk and temporary file will only
be in the page cache, after this change you pay for allocating, writing
back, and deleting the temporary file.

---

But I'm not sure how you imagine this would work without restricting
writeback to particular inodes. Maybe we could mark inodes which have
folios with dropbehind set and make flush worker only write such inodes?
That would probably require tracking number of folios with dropbehind flag
per inode but that wouldn't be too bad. Or we could just lazily clear this
"inode has dropbehind folios" flag once the inode is clean.

								Honza

-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

reviews.author: Christoph Hellwig
reviews.summary: Reviewer suggested modifying the patch to expedite writes by the number of pending don't-cache folios, which would still prioritize writing the least recently dirtied inodes.
reviews.raw_body:
I'd only expedite writing by the number of pending dontcache folios.
It would still write the least recently dirtied inodes.

2026-04-09.patch_summary: This patch set improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by kicking the flusher thread for the superblock instead of initiating writeback directly. This change is based on benchmarking results that show a clear improvement over the status quo, particularly in cases with heavy write activity. The patch also includes a comparison of different IO modes for nfsd and highlights the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE with write-heavy workloads. The approach taken involves modifying the generic_write_sync function to kick the flusher thread, which is expected to better isolate the DONTCACHE writeback behavior and improve overall performance.

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary: Reviewer Jan Kara noted that the patch may cause dontcache folios to stay cached for a long time if mixed with normal writes, but agreed that it might level out over longer periods.
reviews.raw_body:
So that may result in dontcache folios staying cached for quite some time
if DONTCACHE and normal writes are mixed. But I agree over longer time it
might just level out so maybe that's fine.

								Honza
-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: Author addressed Jan Kara's concern that RWF_DONTCACHE on writes provides guarantees about when the writeback will be done, explaining it only means 'don't cache this data beyond what's needed for writeback'. No fix is planned.
reviews.raw_body:
I think that's probably OK. In my interpretation, DONTCACHE on write
just means "don't cache this data beyond what's needed for writeback".
It doesn't provide any guarantees about when the writeback will be
done.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

2026-04-10.patch_summary: This patch set improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by kicking the flusher thread for the superblock instead of initiating writeback directly. This change is based on benchmarking results that show a clear improvement over the status quo, particularly in cases with heavy write activity. The patch also includes a comparison of different IO modes for nfsd and highlights the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE with write-heavy workloads. The approach taken involves modifying the generic_write_sync function to kick the flusher thread, which is expected to better isolate the DONTCACHE writeback behavior and improve overall performance.

reviews.author: Jeff Layton (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed Ritesh's concern about the performance of RWF_DONTCACHE in write-heavy workloads, specifically that it was worse than just kicking the flusher thread. The author drafted a version of the patch to allow the flusher thread to only flush a single inode, but found that this approach had better performance than today's DONTCACHE but worse than just kicking the flusher thread. The author decided not to pursue this approach due to losing batching opportunities.

2026-04-16.patch_summary: This patch set improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by kicking the flusher thread for the superblock instead of initiating writeback directly. This change is based on benchmarking results that show a clear improvement over the status quo, particularly in cases with heavy write activity. The patch also includes a comparison of different IO modes for nfsd and highlights the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE with write-heavy workloads. The approach taken involves modifying the generic_write_sync function to kick the flusher thread, which is expected to better isolate the DONTCACHE writeback behavior and improve overall performance.

reviews.author: Ritesh (IBM)
reviews.summary: Ritesh suggested an alternative approach to Christoph's suggestion, proposing to track the number of dontcache pages and kick the flusher for those many target pages, which would still reduce dirty page cache pressure without necessarily writing only dontcache marked folios.
reviews.raw_body:
So, if I understood it correctly, Christoph might be talking about a
different approach here.
Instead of kicking flusher thread to writeback pages for a single inode,
if we can track the number of dontcache pages
(get_nr_dontcache_pages()), then we can kick the flusher for those many
target pages. I think this way we are still reducing the dirty page
cache pressure - the problem which RWF_DONTCACHE is supposed to solve.
But I guess, that doesn't necessarily always mean that only dontcache
marked folios will get written.

If we implement that then, this should still help with the batching
problem you mentioned and hopefully should not cause a major regression
for the workload which Jan mentioned.

Feel free to correct my understanding here please.

-ritesh

2026-04-17.patch_summary: This patch set improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by kicking the flusher thread for the superblock instead of initiating writeback directly. This change is based on benchmarking results that show a clear improvement over the status quo, particularly in cases with heavy write activity. The patch also includes a comparison of different IO modes for nfsd and highlights the poor performance of RWF_DONTCACHE with write-heavy workloads. The approach taken involves modifying the generic_write_sync function to kick the flusher thread, which is expected to better isolate the DONTCACHE writeback behavior and improve overall performance.
